title: 08-3-2 Henry Bernstein - Deposition, Erosion and Corrosion of Biomass Pyrolysis Fuels
text: 1 DEPOSITION, EROSION AND CORROSION OF BIOMASS PYROLYSIS FUELS FOR INDUSTRIAL GAS TURBINES by Henry L. Bernstein, Ph.D. for Gas Turbine Materials and Component Lifing Challenges and Opportunities in Future Gas Turbine Development and Operation National Research Council Canada Ottawa, Ontario October 21, 2008 401 Isom Road, #570, San Antonio, TX 78216 voice: 210-342-8866 -- fax: 210-342-8935 -- e-mail: gtma@texas.net GAS TURBINE MATERIALS ASSOCIATES 2 OUTLINE z Introduction z Erosion z Deposition z Corrosion – Hot Corrosion z Fuel Requirements and Alternate Fuels z Materials and Coatings z Life Prediction z Summary G AS TURBINE M ATERIALS A SSOCIATES 3 INTRODUCTION G AS TURBINE M ATERIALS A SSOCIATES 4 OBJECTIVES z Background of DEC -- Deposition, Erosion and Corrosion z Discuss Fuel Requirements and Alternate Fuels z Discuss Impact of Corrosion on Materials and Coatings z Briefly Discuss Life Prediction for DEC and Life Management Systems G AS TURBINE M ATERIALS A SSOCIATES 5 TALK BASED UPON “Impact Study on the Use of Biomass-Derived Fuels in Gas Turbines for Power Generation,” Final Report prepared for the National Renewable Energy Laboratory under Contract AV-2-12148-1, June 1993 “Fuel-Specification Considerations For Biomass Pyrolysis Liquids to be Used in Stationary Gas Turbines” ASME Paper 96-GT-406 Co-Author Dr. Clifford A. Moses G AS TURBINE M ATERIALS A SSOCIATES 6 EROSION G AS TURBINE M ATERIALS A SSOCIATES 7 EROSION Erosion is the Removal of Metal by Abrasive Particles in the Gas Stream. It is Controlled by: • Hardness of Abrasive Particles • Size and Shape

text: 96-GT-406 Co-Author Dr. Clifford A. Moses G AS TURBINE M ATERIALS A SSOCIATES 6 EROSION G AS TURBINE M ATERIALS A SSOCIATES 7 EROSION Erosion is the Removal of Metal by Abrasive Particles in the Gas Stream. It is Controlled by: • Hardness of Abrasive Particles • Size and Shape of Abrasive Particles • Number of Abrasive Particles • Angle of Attack – Have all angles of interest in the turbine section G AS TURBINE M ATERIALS A SSOCIATES 8 EROSION OF A TRAILING EDGE VANE z Can Reduce Performance by Enlarging the Flow Path z Can Lead to Cracking Due to Thinning and Grooving G AS TURBINE M ATERIALS A SSOCIATES 50 MW Gas Turbine 9 EROSION OF COATINGS Erosion Can Remove Protective Coatings – Corrosion – Oxidation Coatings – Thermal Barrier Coatings Results in Loss of Protection G AS TURBINE M ATERIALS A SSOCIATES 10 DEPOSITION G AS TURBINE M ATERIALS A SSOCIATES 11 DEPOSITION Deposition Is the Buildup of Foreign Material on the Airfoils It is Controlled by: • Stickiness of Particles • Size of Particles • Number of Particles • Angle of Attack – Have all angles of interest in the turbine section G AS TURBINE M ATERIALS A SSOCIATES 12 DEPOSITION G AS TURBINE M ATERIALS A SSOCIATES Particle Trajectories Vane Blade Percent Striking Surface (mass ratio of particles striking surface to particles in gas) 0 20 40 60 80 100 0 5 10 15 Particle Diameter (microns) Blade Vane Sticking vs. Particle Diameter 13

title: 08-3-2 Henry Bernstein - Deposition, Erosion and Corrosion of Biomass Pyrolysis Fuels
text: M ATERIALS A SSOCIATES 12 DEPOSITION G AS TURBINE M ATERIALS A SSOCIATES Particle Trajectories Vane Blade Percent Striking Surface (mass ratio of particles striking surface to particles in gas) 0 20 40 60 80 100 0 5 10 15 Particle Diameter (microns) Blade Vane Sticking vs. Particle Diameter 13 DEPOSITION z Reduces Performance – Can Be Drastic z Can Reduce Blade Creep Life by Increasing Blade Weight (i.e. Stress) 14 CORROSION HOT CORROSION G AS TURBINE M ATERIALS A SSOCIATES 15 TEMPERATURE REGIMES OF HIGH TEMPERATURE ATTACK G AS TURBINE M ATERIALS A SSOCIATES 16 HOT CORROSION (1 of 4) LOW TEMPERATURE HOT CORROSION (TYPE II) HIGH TEMPERATURE HOT CORROSION (TYPE I) G AS TURBINE M ATERIALS A SSOCIATES 17 HOT CORROSION (2 of 4) z High Temperature Hot Corrosion (Type I) – 1500 to 1750°F (815 to 955°C) – Maximum Between 1550 to 1700°F (845 to 925°C) – Due to Molten Alkali Sulfates Reacting with Protective Oxide Scale – Broad, Relatively Uniform Attack of the Metal (and Coating) z Low Temperature Hot Corrosion (Type II) – 1100 to 1500°F (675 to 815°C) – Maximum Between 1259 to 1350°F (675 to 730°C) – Due to Molten Alkali Sulfates Reacting with Protective Oxide Scale – Need High Amounts of SO3 to Occur at Low Temperatures – Non-Uniform, Pitting Attack of the Metal (and Coating) 18 HOT CORROSION (3 of 4) z Caused by Contaminants – Sulfur PLUS – Sodium and/or Potassium – Form Sodium and Potassium Sulfates z Contaminants Come

title: 08-3-2 Henry Bernstein - Deposition, Erosion and Corrosion of Biomass Pyrolysis Fuels
text: with Protective Oxide Scale – Need High Amounts of SO3 to Occur at Low Temperatures – Non-Uniform, Pitting Attack of the Metal (and Coating) 18 HOT CORROSION (3 of 4) z Caused by Contaminants – Sulfur PLUS – Sodium and/or Potassium – Form Sodium and Potassium Sulfates z Contaminants Come from – Fuel – Air – Water / Steam • NOx Control • Power Augmentation G AS TURBINE M ATERIALS A SSOCIATES 19 HOT CORROSION (4 of 4) z Lead Induced Hot Corrosion (Lead Oxides) z Vanadium Induced Hot Corrosion (Vanadium Pentoxide) z Mixed Hot Corrosion (Chemical Soup) G AS TURBINE M ATERIALS A SSOCIATES 20 EFFECT OF SULFUR ON CORROSION G AS TURBINE M ATERIALS A SSOCIATES 0 20 40 60 80 100 120 140 1 10 100 1000 Aluminide Coating on Mar M-246 Sulfur In Fuel, ppm 80 Hours 50 Hours Weight Loss, mg/cm2 21 FUEL REQUIREMENTS and ALTERNATE FUELS G AS TURBINE M ATERIALS A SSOCIATES 22 TYPICAL FUEL REQUIREMENTS G AS TURBINE M ATERIALS A SSOCIATES Fuel Property Frame Aeroderivative Purpose Ash, % mass, max 0.01% NS Deposition Particulates, mg/l NS 2.6 Deposition and Erosion Sulfur, %mass, max 0.3% 1.0% Emissions Sulfur, ppm 3,000 10,000 Trace Metals ppm wt, max Hot Corrosion Na + K 0.5 0.2 Ca 0.5 2.0 Pb 0.5 1.0 V 0.5 0.5 23 TRACE METALS G AS TURBINE M ATERIALS A SSOCIATES Amount of Trace Metals Allowed Is the Sum from All Sources Fuel + Air + Water/Steam 24 ALTERNATE FUELS Biomass

text: Metals ppm wt, max Hot Corrosion Na + K 0.5 0.2 Ca 0.5 2.0 Pb 0.5 1.0 V 0.5 0.5 23 TRACE METALS G AS TURBINE M ATERIALS A SSOCIATES Amount of Trace Metals Allowed Is the Sum from All Sources Fuel + Air + Water/Steam 24 ALTERNATE FUELS Biomass Pyrolysis Oils: • Switchgrass Oils • Oakwood Oils • Pine Oils • Hawker Siddley Pyrolysis Oils G AS TURBINE M ATERIALS A SSOCIATES 25 BIOMASS PYROLYSIS OILS G AS TURBINE M ATERIALS A SSOCIATES Property Typical #2 Fuel Switchgrass Oakwood Pine Hawker Siddley A B Sulfur, wt% 0.1-0.3 0.03-0.04 0.01-0.03 --- 0.01 0.01 Sulfur, ppm 1000-3000 300-400 100-300 --- 100 100 Alkali, ppm Na <0.5 20-40 10-52 10 39.5 74.8 K <0.5 580-1380 <10-40 <10 8.0 4.2 Ca <0.5 490-660 23-80 60 36.0 15.1 Ash, wt% 0.01 0.12-0.95 <0.05-0.05 0.05 0.1 --- LHV, MJ/kg ~ 42 21.9-23.1 19.6-28.7 21.4 --- --- 26 BIOMASS PYROLYSIS OILS z Sulfur: ~10x Lower than Typical Fuel z Alkali: 10 to 1000x Greater than Typical Fuel z Ash: 5 to 100x Greater than Typical Fuel z LHV (Lower Heating Valve): ~2x Lower than Typical Fuel – Need more fuel for same power – Therefore, allowable contaminates must be lower G AS TURBINE M ATERIALS A SSOCIATES 27 MATERIALS AND COATINGS G AS TURBINE M ATERIALS A SSOCIATES 28 SUPERALLOYS Nickel Base z Higher Strength z Blades, Vanes and Combustor Hardware z Chromium – Hot Corrosion and Oxidation Protection z Aluminum – Oxidation Protection z Normally

title: 08-3-2 Henry Bernstein - Deposition, Erosion and Corrosion of Biomass Pyrolysis Fuels
text: contaminates must be lower G AS TURBINE M ATERIALS A SSOCIATES 27 MATERIALS AND COATINGS G AS TURBINE M ATERIALS A SSOCIATES 28 SUPERALLOYS Nickel Base z Higher Strength z Blades, Vanes and Combustor Hardware z Chromium – Hot Corrosion and Oxidation Protection z Aluminum – Oxidation Protection z Normally Coated for Corrosion and Oxidation Protection z Frame GT’s – Higher Cr and Greater Corrosion Resistance z Aeroderivative GTs’ – Lower Cr and Higher Strength Cobalt Base z Greater Corrosion Resistance z Vanes z Chromium – Hot Corrosion and Oxidation Protection z May or May Not Be Coated z Same Alloys for Frame and Aeroderivative G AS TURBINE M ATERIALS A SSOCIATES 29 COATINGS (1 OF 2) Surface Layer on Blades and Vanes Enriched in : • Chromium • Aluminum • Other Elements Added to Improve Resistance to Attack – Silicon – Resists Type II Hot Corrosion – Platinum – Resists Type I Hot Corrosion Provides Increased Resistance to Hot Corrosion and Oxidation G AS TURBINE M ATERIALS A SSOCIATES 30 COATINGS (2 OF 2) Aluminides z Simple Aluminides – Ni-Aluminides – CoAl z Platinum Aluminides – 2 Phase – 1 Phase z Silicon Modified Aluminides z Other Modified Aluminides z Usually applied by pack, CVD or slurry methods Overlays z MCrAlY’s z M = Ni, Co or Ni+Co z Cr z Al z Y – ties up sulfur, increasing scale adherence z Other elements sometimes added z Usually applied by thermal spray G AS TURBINE M ATERIALS A SSOCIATES

title: 08-3-2 Henry Bernstein - Deposition, Erosion and Corrosion of Biomass Pyrolysis Fuels
text: z Usually applied by pack, CVD or slurry methods Overlays z MCrAlY’s z M = Ni, Co or Ni+Co z Cr z Al z Y – ties up sulfur, increasing scale adherence z Other elements sometimes added z Usually applied by thermal spray G AS TURBINE M ATERIALS A SSOCIATES Separate Metallurgical System Many Different Types of Coatings and Application Methods 31 THERMAL BARRIER COATINGS z Ceramic Layer on the Outside of the Blade or Vane z Ceramic Layer Is an Insulator – Lowers the Metal Temperature – Requires a Heat Sink z Lower Temperature Increases the Blade Life temp. without TBC temp. with TBC T Hot Gases Cooling Air TBC Blade Wall G AS TURBINE M ATERIALS A SSOCIATES 32 AIR PLASMA SPRAY THERMAL BARRIER COATING Ceramic Layer of TBC on Top APS Bond Coat on Bottom Ceramic – Yittria Stabilized Zirconia Bond Coat - Overlay Base Metal G AS TURBINE M ATERIALS A SSOCIATES 33 LIFE PREDICTION G AS TURBINE M ATERIALS A SSOCIATES 34 LIFE PREDICTION FOR EROSION AND DEPOSITION IN IGT’s z Erosion Is Typically Not Lifed z Source(s) of Erosion Are Eliminated z Deposition Is a Performance Issue and Not a Lifing Issue z Deposition is Typically Handled by Either: – Removing the Source(s) of Deposition – Empirically Determining Appropriate Cleaning Intervals G AS TURBINE M ATERIALS A SSOCIATES 35 LIFE PREDICTION FOR HOT CORROSION z Hot Corrosion Is Typically Not Lifed z Source(s) of Hot Corrosion Are Eliminated z Base Metals and Coatings Typically

title: 08-3-2 Henry Bernstein - Deposition, Erosion and Corrosion of Biomass Pyrolysis Fuels
text: is Typically Handled by Either: – Removing the Source(s) of Deposition – Empirically Determining Appropriate Cleaning Intervals G AS TURBINE M ATERIALS A SSOCIATES 35 LIFE PREDICTION FOR HOT CORROSION z Hot Corrosion Is Typically Not Lifed z Source(s) of Hot Corrosion Are Eliminated z Base Metals and Coatings Typically Can Withstand Hot Corrosion Conditions for Only a Short Time – 2,000 to 8,000 Hours Before Parts Begin to Fail G AS TURBINE M ATERIALS A SSOCIATES 36 TBC’s AND HOT CORROSION z TBC’s Can Not Tolerate Hot Corrosion Conditions z The Liquid Sulfates Wick into the Ceramic, Causing It to Spall Off – Difference in Thermal Expansion Between Sulfate and Ceramic G AS TURBINE M ATERIALS A SSOCIATES 37 LIFE MANAGEMENT SYSTEMS z First Stage Nozzle z Cracking z First Stage Bucket / Blade z Creep z TMF z GE MS7001 Gas Turbine – Models B, E & EA z RB211-24C HPT Blade – TMF z Compute Life Consumed and Life Remaining Based Upon Engine Operation / Metallurgical Exam 38 Computer Program STRUCTURE OF THE LIFE MANAGEMENT SYSTEM Metallurgical Examination 39 RESULTS of LIFE MANAGEMENT SYSTEM z First Stage Nozzle z Cracking Predicted to Within a Factor of 2 – Material Variation z First Stage Bucket z Creep Predicted, but No Creep Failures or Cracks / Voids z TMF Crack Initiation Predicted to Within a Factor of 2 – Mat'l Var z Has Saved Operators Millions of Dollars z Longer Operation of Engine & Components – Direct Benefit z

title: 08-3-2 Henry Bernstein - Deposition, Erosion and Corrosion of Biomass Pyrolysis Fuels
text: – Material Variation z First Stage Bucket z Creep Predicted, but No Creep Failures or Cracks / Voids z TMF Crack Initiation Predicted to Within a Factor of 2 – Mat'l Var z Has Saved Operators Millions of Dollars z Longer Operation of Engine & Components – Direct Benefit z Improved Knowledge of Components and Engines – Indirect Benefit 40 SUMMARY G AS TURBINE M ATERIALS A SSOCIATES 41 THE KEY IS TO KEEP DEC MANAGABLE z Deposition and Erosion – Keep Ash and Particulates Very Low z Corrosion – Keep Alkali Sulfates, Lead and Vanadium Very, Very Low – Lower Sulfur in Alternate Fuels May Allow Higher Alkali Metals – Mix Higher Alkali Fuels into Fuels with few Alkalis z NO Magical Material Solutions z NO Real Life Prediction Solutions for DEC G AS TURBINE M ATERIALS A SSOCIATES 42 COMPONENT LIFE PREDICTION z Feasible – Nozzles – Blades – Creep – Thermal Mechanical Fatigue z Has Been Used – Saved Millions of Dollars G AS TURBINE M ATERIALS A SSOCIATES 43 DISCUSSION QUESTIONS 1. Can alkali levels be raised if sulfur is lowered ? 2. Does the future use of alternate fuels require significant improvements in base metals and coatings ? These may not occur ! 3. Can TBC’s be developed to resist hot corrosion ? 4. What is the value of accurate component life prediction for gas turbine operators ? – Industrial – Civil Aviation – Military Aviation – Marine – Military (Civil) G AS TURBINE M
